mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the founding, development, and political and social life of the 13 English colonies in North America (C.3).
Spanish discovery, exploration, and conquest of the Caribbean, Mexico, Central America, and Peru from 1492 to c. 1550 (B.1).
Spanish conquest of Mexico and the destruction of the Aztec state provide context for Spain's colonial empire in the Americas (B.1).
Spanish colonization of the Yucatan Peninsula and Central America involved the subjugation of Maya peoples (B.1).
The section traces colonial self-government, loose royal control, and growing dissatisfaction that set the stage for the American Revolution (C.3.b-c).
The political culture of colonial America, including self-government and the American Philosophical Society, formed the Founders' milieu (C.3.b-c).
Spanish, Portuguese, English, French, and Dutch colonial empires in the Americas exemplify the varieties of European imperial expansion (A-D).
British mercantilism, the Navigation Acts, and development of the English colonies in North America are covered (C.3.b.i).
The introduction of slavery into the colonies, African slave trade, and Indian slavery under the encomienda are discussed (B.2.b, C.3.b.iii).
The literary and cultural production of Spain's colonial empire and Portuguese Brazil form the corpus of colonial Latin American literature (B-D).
Indian policy including slavery under the encomienda, the missionary role of the Church, and conflicts with the French and Indians are covered (B.2.b, C.3.d).
Norse voyages to Greenland, early English exploration, and French exploration of North America are explicitly discussed (C.1-C.2, D).
Mercantilism, the Navigation Acts, colonial economic exploitation, and growth of agricultural and mining economies reflect early capitalist development (B.2.c, C.3.b.i).
Puritan settlements in New England were among the most significant English colonies, established for religious reasons (C.3.a).
The exploration and colonization of Brazil by the Portuguese, including colonial policies, slavery, and administration (B.3).
The introduction of black slavery in Spanish and English colonies provides historical context for abolitionist movements (B.2.b, C.3.b.iii).
Voyages of discovery and colonial trade routes across the Atlantic depended on advances in navigation (C.1).
Religious reasons for colonial settlement, the Great Awakening, and the beginning of religious diversity in the colonies are discussed (C.3.a, C.3.c).
Immigration and the rise of economic classes in the colonies, including the diverse origins of colonial settlers (C.3.b.iii).
Colonial mercantilism, gold and silver mining, agricultural development, and smuggling and piracy connect to global economic patterns (B.2.c).
Norse voyages to Greenland and North America (c. 1000) are explicitly discussed as the earliest European contact with the Americas (C.1).
The forced diaspora of enslaved Africans to the Americas and voluntary emigration of Europeans constitute major diasporic movements (B.2.b, C.3.b.iii).
The colonial structures of European empires in the Americas including encomienda, slavery, and mercantilism are subjects of postcolonial analysis (B.2).
